1: Solomon strengthened himself (wayyitkazzēq) on his throne as the Davidic heir — the same verb YHWH uses when charging Joshua and the same David used when charging Solomon (1 Chronicles 22:13). The Chronicler's opening frames Solomon's reign as a continuation of the covenant-strengthening that is always YHWH's gift to the heir. In Christ the pattern is fulfilled: the Davidic Son who is strengthened through resurrection and enthroned at the right hand (Ephesians 1:20-21), all Israel's king from the first moment of his establishment.
2: 'Solomon spoke to all Israel' — the Chronicler consistently presents the Davidic king as addressing the unified covenant community. Christ as the fulfillment of Solomon addresses 'all Israel' in the eschatological sense: calling the lost sheep of Israel first (Matthew 10:6), then the nations, gathering the one flock under one shepherd (John 10:16; Ephesians 2:11-22).
3: The pilgrimage to the high place at Gibeon — the legitimate sanctuary where Moses' tent of meeting still stood with Bezalel's bronze altar. The Chronicler's careful note about why Gibeon was lawful (2 Chronicles 1:4: the ark was in Jerusalem) explains the divided worship geography before the temple. Christ transcends both Gibeon and Jerusalem: 'the hour is coming when you will worship the Father neither on this mountain nor in Jerusalem' (John 4:21), because in him all worship is now gathered.
4: David had brought the ark to Jerusalem; but the bronze altar of Bezalel remained at Gibeon. The separation of ark and altar before Solomon's temple is a divided-glory state the temple will resolve. Christ unites what was divided: in his body both the presence of God (ark) and the sacrifice (altar) converge — 'the Lamb who is the lamp of it' (Revelation 21:23), both priest and sacrifice at once.
5: The bronze altar Bezalel made stands at Gibeon — connecting Solomon's worship directly to Moses' tabernacle tradition. The bronze altar is the place where the covenant's atonement logic was enacted. Christ is the final bronze altar: 'we have an altar from which those who serve the tent have no right to eat' (Hebrews 13:10), the sacrifice that supersedes every previous altar.
6: Solomon offered a thousand burnt offerings on the bronze altar — the massive scale of his worship before receiving wisdom. The generosity of Solomon's sacrifice precedes the gift. Christ offered himself once for all (Hebrews 7:27) — not a thousand sacrifices but one of infinite worth, the single offering that perfects forever those who are sanctified (Hebrews 10:14).
7: YHWH appeared to Solomon at night with the open offer: 'Ask what I shall give you.' The unrestricted divine offer is the grace-form of sovereign generosity. Christ makes the same unrestricted offer in John 14:13-14: 'Whatever you ask in my name, this I will do.' The pattern — divine offer, human request, gracious grant — is fulfilled when prayer is made in the name of the one greater than Solomon (Matthew 12:42).
8: Solomon begins by acknowledging YHWH's ḥesed to David: 'you have shown great steadfast love to David my father.' Before asking anything, he locates himself within the covenant history. Christ is both the recipient and the fullness of this covenant loyalty — 'the sure mercies of David' (Isaiah 55:3; Acts 13:34), the one in whom all of YHWH's ḥesed to the Davidic line finds its irreversible confirmation.
9: 'Let your word to David my father now be confirmed.' Solomon's prayer appeals to the Davidic promise as its foundation. The promise is confirmed not in Solomon's temple but in the resurrection of Christ — Peter's Pentecost sermon explicitly makes this link: God swore to David that 'one of his descendants' would sit on his throne, 'he foresaw and spoke about the resurrection of the Christ' (Acts 2:30-31).
10: 'Give me wisdom and knowledge that I may lead out and bring in this great people of yours.' Solomon's request — wisdom for servant-leadership, not personal advantage — is the type of Christ's incarnational purpose. 'In him are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge' (Colossians 2:3): the wisdom Solomon received in part, Christ possesses in fullness. The servant-request also prefigures the principle Jesus states in Matthew 20:26-28: greatness in his kingdom is measured by service.
11: YHWH commends Solomon for not asking for riches, honor, long life, or the death of enemies — for choosing wisdom for others over goods for himself. Christ fulfills the antitype fully: he 'did not count equality with God a thing to be grasped, but emptied himself' (Philippians 2:6-7), choosing the servant-form. The one who had all chose the posture of not-grasping.
12: 'Wisdom and knowledge are granted to you, and I will also give you riches, possessions, and honor.' The sequence — seek first the kingdom-purpose, and all other things are added — is the pattern of Matthew 6:33. Solomon receives the horizontal goods (riches, honor) as consequence of the vertical choice (wisdom). Christ is the one who 'became poor so that you by his poverty might become rich' (2 Corinthians 8:9) — riches given through self-emptying, not through self-seeking.
13: Solomon returned from Gibeon to Jerusalem, from the tent of meeting to the throne. The transition from tent-worship to enthroned rule marks the beginning of the temple period. Christ makes the analogous transition in Hebrews 10:12-13: from offering himself (tent-sacrifice) to sitting at the right hand (enthronement), 'waiting until his enemies are made a footstool.'
14: Solomon's chariot and horsemen accumulation begins the description of his material wealth. Deuteronomy 17:16 warned the future king not to multiply horses. Solomon's gathering prefigures the kingdom-of-this-world temptation that Christ refused in the wilderness (Matthew 4:8-10): all the kingdoms and their glory, offered and declined. The true king's power is not in chariots (Psalms 20:7).
15: Silver and gold made common as stones in Jerusalem — the prosperity of the Solomonic age anticipating the new creation's abundance: 'its gates will never be shut by day' (Revelation 21:25); 'the kings of the earth will bring their glory into it.' The new Jerusalem fulfills what Solomon's Jerusalem only sketched.
16: Horses imported from Egypt — Solomon's commerce with Egypt signals the beginning of his entanglement with the world-system Deuteronomy warned against (2 Chronicles 17:16-17). Christ alone navigates the temptation without capitulation: he does not accumulate the world's symbols of power; 'my kingdom is not of this world' (John 18:36).
17: The pricing list for horses and chariots — Solomon as international broker between Egypt and the Hittite and Aramean kings. Christ is the true broker between heaven and earth, the one mediator (1 Timothy 2:5) through whom all divine gifts are mediated — not for commercial exchange but through self-giving.
